pathophysiology_summary: Tension-type headache is the commonest primary headache: a bilateral, pressing/tightening ('band-like'), non-pulsatile, mild-to-moderate headache not aggravated by routine activity, without nausea and with at most one of photophobia/phonophobia, often with pericranial muscle tenderness and provoked by stress/fatigue/poor posture. It is benign and self-limiting. The overriding ground-truth task is red-flag screening for secondary headache (SNNOOP-type flags): thunderclap/'worst-ever' sudden onset (subarachnoid haemorrhage), fever/neck stiffness/rash (meningitis), focal neurology/altered consciousness, new headache with immunosuppression or cancer, positional/valsalva/worse-on-waking pattern (raised intracranial pressure), and, in those >50, jaw claudication/scalp tenderness/visual loss (giant cell arteritis), plus hypertensive emergency. With red flags absent, management is simple analgesia (limiting frequency to avoid medication-overuse headache), addressing stress/posture/screen triggers, and neck/relaxation measures.

differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral pressing band-like pain, not throbbing, not activity-aggravated, no nausea, stress-triggered.

differential.diagnosis: Subarachnoid haemorrhage / other serious secondary headache
differential.evidence_basis: New severe headache must exclude thunderclap onset, meningitis, focal neurology, raised ICP, GCA, hypertensive emergency.
Unilateral throbbing, nausea, photophobia and activity aggravation would suggest migraine — absent here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Tension-type headache may lead, but secondary-headache red flags must be actively excluded before reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: No secondary-headache red flags, classic tension-type features and pericranial tenderness — primary tension-type headache.
No red flags; escalation target if any develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative red-flag screen, tension-type headache leads; simple analgesia + trigger management, with a thunderclap/meningism/focal-deficit safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; benign; address stress/posture and avoid analgesic overuse.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign tension-type headache.

red_flags.description: Secondary-headache red flags — thunderclap/worst-ever sudden onset, fever/neck stiffness/rash, focal neurology/altered consciousness, new headache with cancer/immunosuppression, positional/valsalva/worse-on-waking pattern, or (>50) jaw claudication/scalp tenderness/visual loss
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicate subarachnoid haemorrhage, meningitis, raised ICP/mass, or giant cell arteritis — emergencies.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reassuring a 'new severe headache' without excluding thunderclap/meningism/focal deficits/GCA.
Encouraging frequent (>2–3 days/week) acute analgesia can cause rebound headaches.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Simple analgesia (limit frequency) + trigger/posture management; 000/ED for thunderclap/meningism/focal deficits; urgent review for GCA features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing subarachnoid haemorrhage, meningitis or GCA behind a 'tension headache'.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Diagnose primary headache after excluding secondary-headache red flags. Tension-type headache: simple analgesia used sparingly (avoid medication-overuse headache), stress/posture management, relaxation. Investigate/escalate red flags.
